top_profane.text:
I really enjoy this TVB release (the sophomore album that doesn't suffer the slump as it goes in very different direction musically than its predecessor, an at times muscular and musically diverse but feet-finding album) but it is not at the top of my list of their albums. The sound recording is very much a product of its time (not really a complaint but you can hear it) and this rock band does something musically very different than their first album: they mellow out and slow down. This takes away a lot of their punch and rawness but they still maintain their emotional energy, stretching it over vulnerable voices and exposing delicate, weak, and yearning hearts. Pritzl invites some guest female vocalists and utilizes them on almost every song. The result ends up being an unusual (and sometimes uncomfortable) mix of two-steps removed from contemporary rock and two-steps removed from contemporary Christian music (though closer to the latter than contemporary rock for the time). Here are some thoughts on each song:

1) There Is No One Like You--a beautiful song full of odd flourishes and subdued but rousing guitar solos. A vibrating female echo and sighs compliment the rich and vulnerable vocals of Michael Pritzl. The title of the song says it all: there is no one like You (God) but for good measure Michael reminds himself (and us) of what he is: "I am nothing!".
top_profane.title: A meditation on the lowliness of our position and the altitude of God's magnificence.

top_profane.text:
An eleven track compilation, 1972's "The Best of Crow" pulled together five out of the band's seven 1969-71 era singles. Missing in action were 1970's 'Slow Down' and 'Watching Can Waste Up Time'. Given the band's limited catalog (they'd only recorded three studio sets up to that point), a "best of" collection struck me as a bit premature, but with lead singer David Wagner having quit the previous year, I'm guessing Amaret wanted to capitalize on the band's audience while they still had a chance. And here's the really funny thing about this album - since the labelt got to "cherry pick" from the first three albums, on a song-for-song basis the results were surprisingly impressive. Yeah, Wagner's voice was an acquire taste (he frequently reminded me of a rock-oriented David Clayton-Thomas), and there wasn't a great deal of originality in these grooves, but looking back I find nine of the tracks score high with my ears (and that includes a couple with horn arrangements).

- Off their debut LP "Crow Music", 'Evil Woman' was their biggest and best hit (really their only hit). Driven by Larry Wiegand's pounding bass and some very daring lyrics (remember this was 1969), the song probably deserved to be a bigger hit than it was. Interestingly, the horns were added without the band's knowledge, or approval, giving the tune a Blood, Sweat & Tears-edge. rating: **** stars
top_profane.title: review

top_profane.text:
Judging by the cover art, liner notes and some of the song titles I initially wondered if I'd purchase a secular album (of course I didn't immediately notice the guy on the cover holding a copy of Rolling Stone). I owned a couple of the earlier Gypsy LPs and they weren't religiously-themed, but people change all the time ...
Co-produced by Jack Richardson and Jim Mason, 1973's "Unlock the Gates" was easily the band's most conventional set. Largely written by lead singer/guitarist Enrico Rosenbaum (singer/guitarist James Johnson and keyboardist James Walsh also contributing material), selections like `Is That News?', the title track and 'Bad Whore (The Machine)' would have sounded perfect slotted into mid-1970s top-40 radio (okay the latter title might have been a problem for commercial radio). Unfortunately while highly commercial, material like `One Step Away' and `Need You Baby' came at the expense of the band's identity. Anyone expecting to hear more of the pseudo-progressive moves found on earlier sets was going to be thoroughly disappointed. To be honest, much of the set could have easily been mistaken for Ambrosia (`Bad Whore'), The Doobie Brothers (`Make Peace with Jesus'), etc. That wasn't necessarily a bad thing, especially if you were into commercial 1970s rock. `Bad Whore (The Machine)' and the funky (yes, funky) `Don't Get Mad (Get Even)' were particular
top_profane.title: Goodbye progressive, hello AOR ...

top_profane.text:
In The Pink is, to my knowledge, the only official release from 80s hair band Dizzy Bitch. It wasn't released on the CD format until fairly recently.

Dizzy Bitch is one of many long-forgotten hair bands that never really made it big, and never achieved any sort of mass popularity. Despite this, and despite the band's relatively short time together, the band had a sound all their own, and it is great to see that this gem, the band's lone release (to my knowledge) has gotten a much-needed reissue. The group features Johnny Lust on drums, these days known around the Vegas Valley for his classic rock tribute band, Three Blind Mice.

There were hundreds of hair bands in the 80s, and Dizzy Bitch scores major points in my book for not being a clone of any of the others. The themes of their music are not unlike other "sleazy" bands of the era, like Faster Pussycat or LA Guns. However, their actual musical sound is drastically different in that it is more toward the "heavy" end of the glam spectrum, and has more in common with a group like Lizzy Borden or Grim Reaper. Hell, these guys are heavier than a lot of the New Wave of British Heavy Metal (NWOBHM) bands I have listened to! The end result is an interesting medium between these two extremes.

After giving the music on this CD a listen (I honestly hadn't heard of these guys until just a few months ago)
top_profane.title: A sleazy classic, given a long overdue reissue!

top_profane.text:
Brown Whörnet is/was a band trumpeting loudly & lewdly from one of the last bastions of creativity, virtuosity and free-thought in "the live music capital of the world" (ie. Austin TX). Stingy Bunion & the Whornets would round-up a half-dozen or more players for each CD or live performance and unleash sonic mayhem with intellectually retarded fury before mostly unsuspecting 6th Streeters who would seek only to shake their hips will sucking on Lone Star lights. Much like their perverted uncles in the Butthole Surfers, BW crank their amps to 11 and with spastic punk ferocity cleave mind-gasms of post-rock
inanities that somehow also simultaneously sound musically like the idiot bastard sons of FZ, as well. Where the Whornets differ from the BHS can be heard in extended workouts of fusion-like, technical heady prog-jams. No small wonder: keyboardist Peter Stopchinsky also performs with Graham Reynolds in more refined jazz & big band projects. Such lofty musicality didn't prevent The Whörnets from embellishing live performances with obtuse on-stage antics & costumery; one New Year's Eve event featured a 2nd set where-in BW dressed as technicolor 1970s disco-heads replete with platform shoes & afros, who then blasted proto-punk versions of Earth Wind & Fire. Such was the live & unpredictable sweetness of a Brown Whörnet concert. This Hamburger disc opens with a rock-n-rol
top_profane.title: Ate it up and burped an epiphany

top_profane.text:
Dick Lourie - Ghost Radio Blues

Very unusual disc here. Dick combined his poetry with blues and early rock styles to create this disc of songs and stories. The musicianship is excellent that accompanies the spoken dialogue and each track has something to say about music history, styles or life. Big Jack Johnson is featured on two tracks which makes sense considering that Dick played sax with Big Jack and his band. Dick is the narrator throughout the disc weaving his poetry and written dialogue into the music and plays some awesome sax along the way. I love the way he plays and it really enhances the proceedings here. Blues at its best is about stories and this is a creative way to expand on that. Well done and recommended for those who enjoy blues or 50’s rock. Take a chance on this if it sounds interesting....you might really appreciate what this disc is all about.
top_profane.title: Excellent disc of stories and poems combined with blues based music

Or..because I belong to it, I guess I forget how small, select, and underground the collective circle of that "WIDELY recognized" is.

Best kept secret, indeed.

But don't take my word for it (though you are about to hear it)! Just look at some of the references to this CD in reviews of other Violet Burning CDs here on Amazon.com (That it is inevitably hinted at, and referenced, in many of these reviews suggests its watershed status). Read respected critics like Kim Gentes: "one of the best worship albums I had ever heard in my life...delicate, evocative and worshipful genius."

Even those of us who were Violet Burning fans from the first (raw, punk-ish) album were unprepared for the intimate and ethereal texture...no, atmosphere..created by this music. Gosh. I can’t remember the last time I listened to a Koda Kumi album. Actually, I think the last one I purchased and listened to was JAPONESQUE. For some reason, her music hadn’t grabbed my attention since then, but after catching snippets of her latest double album release, specifically the Outside version, I must say I’m loving the sound of Koda Kumi once again. Maybe it’s the fact that I’m more into her bouncy dance tracks and the entire Outside record sounds like the soundtrack of my life at the moment. Whatever the case, the Pop Diva is back and totally killing it. Check out the snippets of both records, packaged separately, and continue with my review of Outside.

Did you feel it? That ounce of excitement that swells from the pit of your stomach and soars to your heart? I know the feeling, it’s exactly what I felt after watching this same teaser and I knew I had to get my hands on both albums. But let’s start with Outside.

The opening slow tempo/groovy track and concept title gets you in the mindset of the direction in which Kumi went with this record. The artist is here in all her confidence and ready to show once more why she’s the “baddest”. W Face explores both sides of Kumi: the part that everyone sees in music videos and on stage, and the other side no one truly knows, the woman behind the music. She sing

top_profane.text:
Writing here as an incidental one-time album "critic," it occurs to me that, given my subject, the only way to do my late bandmate Lester Bangs' only album justice, is to do so in words that read as close in style and degree of sheer audacity as his own. For those either too young or just not paying attention at the time, the late Lester Bangs was, throughout the 70s, truly America's first, and arguably only, Rock Star Journalist. When fired from Rolling Stone Magazine for being "too cruel to musicians" in record reviews, he migrated to Detroit to, as it were, become the very heart of Creem Magazine. Then onto NYC, where he finally put his money where his mouth was and started his own band. I met and briefly played drums for him shortly thereafter.
As "Jook Savages" is the LP he recorded in Texas following the self-destruction of his New York outfit Birdland, I in fact have never actually heard it. I don't have to - I knew Lester, knew how bad he smelled most of the time, drank with him, played his music with him and, on one auspicious occasion, not only set a poem of his to music, but had the resulting song "Loneliness" singled out by none other than John Cale as his favourite of all our tunes (Cale subsequently paid all my train fare, drumstick and drum head expenses for my rehearsals). Having lived inside Lester's raw-nerved music (and otherworldly stench) as a
top_profane.title: Rock Critic Turns Rock Artist Turns Stone Dead and You Probably Weren't There

top_profane.text: This book was truly awful.  Where to begin?  The plot was lame and predictable. Virtually the entire book is process-oriented, centering on a run involving Nick and an artifact he stole.  There is so much filler and waste.  For example, there is an entire chapter devoted to Rachel trying to get a random dude to let her use scuba diving equipment.  Perhaps the worst aspect of the book is that there is little to no development of the main characters and their relationships.  If you're a fan of Rachel/Kisten, don't bother with this book because their interaction is extremely limited. If you are a fan of Rachel/Ivy, don't bother with this book because its events do more to hinder their relationship than enhance it.  You may want to read a summary because the only significant thing that happens in this book involves their relationship, but it's not worth wading through all the crap to get to the couple important scenes.  I don't know if Nick/Rachel fans exist, but if they do, don't bother with this book because the storyline will not make you happy.  The only relationship that is marginally improved in this book is the relationship between Rachel and Jenks.  Also, the book is full of typos. top_rant.text:
Hah! OK..

Is it possible to literally shout and rave like a madfan over what is essentially just relaxing quiet ambient?!?!

Id say so!

Uruk-Hai is essentially new-age music for the black metal world. Although many people care next to nothing for this highly proliferous project, this one man ambient band lead by the chainmail clad Hugin has been able to do something that no other metal artist has truly been able to achieve for me.

I look for metal to take me away to mystical worlds I've created in my mind, or to memories of natural landscapes I've explored. Music has served for me what fantasy novels or video games have done for so many others. And even in my earliest of days that is what I got out of my first heavy metal cds from Tiamat and Type O Negative. It has been for me the wellspring of inspiration and spiritual refreshment. Uruk-Hai came to me after looking through his long discography of fantasy themed ambient. I got a few cd-rs from this group, which I enjoyed for the most part. But it was with the demo tape "The Lord of the Rings" that I found a recording capable of actually making me feel like I was really there! That I was really IN the world I envisioned in my mind! The music was EXACTLY like the landscape on the tape's cover! It probably will later become one of the most inspirational experiences of my life.
top_rant.title: GREATEST FANTASY AMBIENT EVER!!

top_rant.text:
Ah, The Sons of Champlin ... talk about a talented band cursed with years of poor choices, personnel hiccups, at least one mental meltdown, mismanagement, corporate indifference, and more than their share of simple bad luck. Most folks don't have a clue these guys are, and if there's name recognition they tend to get lumped in with the rest of the San Francisco psych mafia - The Airplane, The Dead, Quicksilver, etc. While they share the same geographic roots and played many of the same clubs and ballrooms, The Sons were on a totally different wavelength. Yeah probably just as stoned, but on a different musical path.

The band's roots trace back to The Opposite Six (one of at least three bands with the same name) which singer/keyboardist Bill Champlin formed while in high school. Drawn by the lure of music Chaplin quit school to pursue music on a professional basis. The band (sax players Ron Arnsmeyer and Tim Cain, Champlin, guitarist Don Irving, bassist Rob Moitoza, and drummer Dick Rogers) actually scored a contract with Dot releasing a lone 1965 single ('All Night Long' b/w 'Come Straight Home' (Dot catalog number 16700) before Moitoza and Rogers fell victim to the draft effectively, killing the group. Champlin and Cain continued their partnership bringing in guitarist Terry Haggerty, drummer Jim Meyers, and former Warlocks/Grateful Dead bassist John Prosser.

Songs like the lead-off track "Ellis In the Orchard" and a couple of instrumental cuts (rare for a Knott release) like "Back To the Orchard" with flowing and heavy guitar lines and major chord sustains are what undoubtedly led to this label but the truth is that the bulk of the album (6 out of the 10 tracks) were varied and ranged from the Red Hot Chili Peppers influenced "A Group of Prophets Predict the Pickers' Future Without Them" (great song BTW) to the edgy hard rocking "She Said" to the playful piano plinks of "The Grape Prophet Speaks". There's hardly been a Knott album that's seen better production values than this one. It's slick, clean, and clear with all of the instruments blending nicely but standing strong in their particular placements. Knott too sounds great and multi-tracked his own vocals as he has often done but, surprisingly, though the album is almost theatrically operatic, he is focused and by comparison to much of his other work is restrained. Vocally he's really serving the individual songs and the story of this concept album.

Yes, you read right. "Attack of the Clones" is my favorite Star Wars movie so far. "A New Hope" and "The Empire Strikes Back" are just as good, but Episode II is the one I enjoy the most.
This movie had everything a die-hard fan of Star Wars could want: Action, suspense, great special effects, great story, and old trilogy forshadowing like what! And lastly, what REAL fan of Star Wars wouldn't want Yoda to fight?! That's the best part of this movie right there! I mean, what more is there to ask for?! This is Star Wars at its best!
For those who complained about the love story, the dialogue and acting didn't sound any different than what I've experienced with young love. When you're young and stupid-happy for someone, all logic fails, and you begin to say corny things, and not act like yourself. I totally saw this in Padme and Anakin. And a lot of the time in real life, young love happens fast like it did in this movie. People go off on Spring break and fall in love all the time. :) And as far as their illogical marriage, you knew this had to happen. But they didn't act illogical at first. They first decided not to fall in love. But after experiencing a life or death situation together, all logic fails. They were already in love. It's just that after that extreme situation they were put in, it lead them to act irrationally and they got married. As far as I'm concerned,
top_rant.title: My favorite Star Wars movie

First off a disclaimer - I didn't buy the set, but I did listen to the entire thing on Spotify using my standard listening setup.
So I haven't written a review for a music CD on Amazon in many many years, but I just felt the need to here.....because the positive reviews (at least the two longer ones at the time of this writing) actually make me legit angry.

Now, I don't claim to be any sort of 'expert'. I'm not even a very good piano player. But I will claim to be a lover of Scott Joplin's music since I was nine years old or so, and I know every piece on this recording intimately. I also has a bachelor's degree in music history, so I certainly know SOMETHING of what I'm talking about.

The main concern with this recording as a whole is that for the most part the tempos are way too slow. Let's go back to the root of the discussion and see what the entire issue is here. In some of the publications of Joplin's rags, there is a notation that states: 'Do not play this piece fast. It is never right to play "Ragtime" fast. Composer'. So what happened is that many people ove the years go it into their heads that ragtime should be played the opposite of fast, that being slow. But the problem with this is that it removes the context of what Joplin was talking about. See, back then, performers would end up playing ragtime (and other music, 'ragging it up'
top_rant.title: Consistently too slow, badly recorded and amateurish playing...people think this is good?

top_screaming.text: COKE ESCOVEDO(joseph thomas escovedo)WAS A LATIN PERCUSSIONIST WHO USED HIS STYLE TO DEFINE A PERIOD IN TIME,BETWEEN THE MID-SIXTYS TO THE EARLY EIGHTIES.COKE WOULD PERFORM WITH OTHER ARTISTS LIKE,SANTANA,HERBIE HANDCOCK,WILLIE BOBO,MALO,PETE ESCOVEDO HIS BROTHER,AND HIS NIECE SHEILA E.COKE,S MORE INTERESTING WORK WOULD BE WITH THE LATIN ROCK GROUP"SANTANA"HE WILL TOUR WITH THE BAND FOR A FEW MONTHS AND HELP WITH SANTANA,S THIRD ALBUM FOR COLUMBIA RECORDS.COKE WITH THE HELP OF HIS BROTHER PETE,WOULD GO ON TO FORM THE GROUP"AZTECA".THE GROUP WILL RELEASE TWO RECORDS,AZTECA AND PYRAMID OF THE MOON.AZTECA WILL FOLD AFTER THE SECOND ALBUM.COKE ESCOVEDO WILL SIGN WITH MERCURY RECORDS AND RELEASE THE RECORD NAMED SIMPLY "COKE".THE ALBUM IS A BLEND OF R&B,LATIN SOUL AND ROCK.TWO OTHER RECORDS WOULD BE COMIN,AT YA!IN 1976 AND DISCO FANTASY,1977.THE TRACK LISTING IS AS FOLLOWS,NO ONE TO DEPEND ON,WHY CAN,T WE BE LOVERS,REBIRTH,EASY COME EASY GO,LOVE LETTERS,HALL,S DELIGHT,IF I EVER LOSE THIS HEAVEN,WHAT ARE YOU UNDER,MAKE IT SWEET AND THE LAST TRACK LIFE IS TORTURED LOVE AFFAIR.THE RECORD IS A MILESTONE FOR THIS GREAT TALENT,AFTER THE RELEASE OF THE THIRD RECORD FOR MERCURY "DISCO FANTASY"WHICH WOULD PROVE A CRITICALLY AND COMMERCIALLY DISAPPOINTING WOULD SIGNAL THE END FOR COKE ESCOVEDO,S CAREER.HE WOULD CONTINUE TO PERFORM WITH MANY OTHER TALENTS.COKE ESCOVEDO,S LIFE WOULD END ON JULY

top_exclaim.text: Back in 1978 or 79? - a lad in my artillery unit in Nuremberg, Germany lent me a cassette tape of a variety of tunes that he thought I might like (as I played (and still play) bagpipes).  One of the tunes was a haunting rendition of the Scottish tune "Johnnie Cope". It was hypnotic, mesmerizing.  I'd never heard anything quite like it.  Parts of it had sort of a middle-eastern air to it.  Strange and Beautiful.  The lad who lent me the tape did not know the name of the performing group; and I certainly did not.  (I was still relatively new to this ilk of music, though I had been searching for true Irish Folk Music since my early teens).  ....So, my goodness - I just HAD to find who these magical artists were.  To make a long story short - when I got back to the States in April of 1980, I began my quest to find this recording basically as soon as I got back to St. Paul, MN.  Within a week(?) I found myself in the "Foreign" Section of the record section in the old (not here anymore) Dayton's Dept. store downtown St. Paul.  I went to the Irish section and began flipping through the LP's, looking for anybody performing the tune "Johnnie Cope".  Suddenly I came upon what my former wife and I (we are still and always will be best friends) would forever call "The Purple Album".  "Hmmm...Johnnie Cope!", I said to myself.  "...Could this be the Group I heard on the tape back in Germany?
top_exclaim.title: One of the BEST Irish Folk Albums Ever.

In fact I'd have to say that, without a doubt, this is up there with Porcupine Tree's excellent Anesthetize and Roger Waters' In the Flesh as among the best live DVDs I own!! And this one isn't even in surround sound!

It is even better than the 7 track bonus DVD that came with the limited edition Anno Domini High Definition CD a few years ago (though all 7 of those songs are on this one too). And, yes, it is a different concert, filmed in a TV studio in front of a small audience (guessing hometown/fanclub type thing).

The first disc is the main concert. The track list is: The Same River, Out of Myself, Volte-Face*, Rainbow Box, 02 Panic Room*, Reality Dream III, I Turned You Down, Dance with the Shadow, Parasomnia*, Second Life Syndrome*, and The Curtain Falls*. And, in case you care, the * are my favorites.

The second disc contains the 2 encores; Before and Ultimate Trip (and this time the entire song, not just the abbreviated first half that's on the ADHD bonus disc) and 7 "bootleg" tracks, recorded in during 2006-2008. They are: Beyond the Eyelids, Loose Heart, Back to the River, Conceiving You, I Believe, Lucid Dream IV, and Reality Dream II. Of course, being bootlegs, they are not up to the quality of the main concert. (And when the heck am I gonna get a live video of Cybernetic Pillow?!?!? Guys - PLEASE put it on a future live DVD!)

Bat Out of Hell is forty-six minutes and thirty-three seconds long and was released on October 21, 1977. Bat Out of Hell reached #9 on the U.S. Billboard 200 Album charts and went 14x Platinum. This was not Meat Loaf debut album. The music on the cassette is long; Bat Out of Hell is the longest at nine minutes and forty-eight seconds. The shortest song is All Revved Up with No Place to Go at four minutes and nineteen seconds; two others songs are over eight minutes long. The music is a story telling rock and roll style. Two out of Three Ain't Bad is a very good example. The song tells the story of a girl breaking up with her boy and explains why she is leaving him. In 1995 The Wu-Tang Clan was still riding high on the runaway success of their debut album, Enter The Wu-Tang (36 Chambers), released two years earlier. It established the nine-member New York rap collective as a dominant force and they quickly built on its success with highly acclaimed solo albums from Method Man, Ol’ Dirty Bastard and Raekwon that only furthered the frenzy among a rabid fan base eager to get their hands on anything Wu-related. GZA (aka The Genius) capitalized on all that forward momentum with Liquid Swords, a solo album which was perhaps the purest distillation of everything that was unique to The Wu-Tang Clan’s corner of the rap world: using strategies from chess and kung-fu as metaphors for lyrical technique, RZA’s stark beats and frequent in-house guest raps from other Clan members. Perhaps the GZA (real name: Gary Grice) stayed so close to the template that RZA had set up for Wu-Tang to operate under because he understood the value of what they had achieved in the industry a little better than the others. After all the Brooklyn native was a few years older than the rest of the Clan, and more experienced too, having already made a solo foray into the rap world with a pre-Wu album, Words From The Genius, released to little notice in 1991.

Fran will really impress you...

This is an album recorded in 1960 for the American Warwick label. The producer was Morton Craft and the orchestra was under the baton of Ralph Burns. This is an exceptional album and came about through her then husband Dick Haymes and their success on the cabaret circuit. Fran was famous for her appearance in the first "Pink Panther" film singing "It Had Better be Tonight".
Although Dick later in his career stated that he did not rate Ralph Burns the treatment of 12 lovely songs for Fran makes the album one you,will want to listen to again and again. She dazzled both vocally and visually.
My favourites are "I'm Gonna Laugh You Out of My Life"; "Out of this World" and "Spring Can Really Hang You Up the Most".
This is musical magic...
Originally recorded as both a mono and stereo album release. This album has been much sought after by collectors for years and as a result may have a premium price.

It isn't just the superb performance of Irtrad tunes ( Irish Traditional Dance Music ) that grabbed my ears, but the lovely atmosphere conveyed in the performance. OK! I was spoiled a long time ago with Galway ( Ireland ) musicians playing that lovely music, but the more I listen to this CD the more I want to listen to it again. In a time when this genre is almost wore out by dilettantes performing it all over the world, the tune images in our heads corrupted, the style become almost deadened by the attempt to "Bluegrass' it with double bass and heavy 2/4 rhythm, this timely publication knocks the crap out of the, well, crap that poses as the real thing, Irtrad.

A refreshing reaffirmation of what I most love, and have always, about this genre. The Irish have a lovely word for the magic that this CD conjures, 'devilment', or fun or just plain magic. It has a bit of the Faerie to it, I guess that today would be Elf or Hobbit, so it belongs in the same place as great stories and books. And even-though we are almost over exposed to great performers in the arena, Mary Bergen for example, Noreen adds the bit that should have made me want to spin Mary's CD over and over; but, I never felt that urge at all, even if Bergen's CD is a masterpiece.

Maybe I am too 'homely' and expect every Irish music CD to fill my head with the aroma of a 'Turf' fire, the taste of 'Bo

If you are coming to this album looking for some more gracious sounds from Foxy Shazam after hearing such albums as The Church of Rock and Roll, or their self titled, you will be very, and hopefully pleasantly, surprised at what you hear on this record.

Although they don't really seem to consider it their first album (hence their Sophomore album, Introducing) as it's more of a compilation of songs from Eric, Sky & Loren's old band, Train of Thought, this album is a hell of a force. The Flamingo Trigger is something of an experimental noise rock post hardcore deal. With screaming and often nonsensical vocals by Eric Nally, mixed with the melodic piano of Sky and raging guitar of Loren, it is a purely impulsive take on what we know as music, and certainly what we know of Foxy Shazam. This album was released long before the band introduced trumpets and theatrical rock anthems as they would in the coming years.

I absolutely love this album. The first song I ever heard from Foxy Shazam was No! Don't shoot, back around 2007, and was immediately hooked. With the greatest breakdown of all time (EE OO AH! BIGGITY BIGGITY BOO!) this album is absolutely mind blowing.

This Flamingo Trigger is a must have for any fans of any experimental rock music and certainly that of any Foxy Shazam fans. Now out of print, it is definitely hard to find...but well worth t

top_five_star_obscene.text:
If you have not gotten any of the editions of Goodbye Lullaby yet, this is the one to buy!! This album has two discs, and it includes all the tracks on the deluxe edition of this album plus:
-What the Hell and Wish You Were Here Instrumentals
-The Making of Goodbye Lullaby
-What the Hell, Smile, Push, Wish You Were Here, and Girlfriend (from 4music Favorites live session)
-What the Hell, Smile and Wish You Were Here music videos
-Behind the scenes of What the Hell and Smile

I highly recommend this album, especially if you're an Avril Lavigne fan, or if you haven't bought any Goodbye Lullaby editions yet since this includes every track, plus bonus tracks/videos on just one album (and the blue color is very pretty)!
This cd is a great addition to your music collection!
top_five_star_obscene.title: The all-in-one album!
